item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Our planet has FTL. And I don't mean our ships.

take this imaginary extra +1 for that line alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I am way, way late to his party, but having the behemoth and Earth both being SCP-4100 (to different groups) is needlessly confusing, especially since the header right after that is "Destruction of SCP-4100". Like, this is around the area where I should have been integrating the implications about the Foundation and the Protectorate and getting wowed by the twist, but instead of getting to enjoy that I ended up having to reread that section for clarity.

It also makes it kind of confusing which "surface" the last message is sending from, and that has drastic impacts on the meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love how these bitches basically yeeted earth at the Destroyer like a massive, FTL baseball.
+1

item.title: Re: Don't use 'Sol' or 'Solar' to describe this star system
item.content:encoded: A few months late to the party, but this is something I overlooked when writing it. I've amended it to "Human Origin System".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh I really loved this one! So unique and fresh. I like the idea of humans not quite understanding things and that’s why SCP’s are contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rigel system

The inside of me began screaming, "STAR TREK!!!!!! ERMAHGERD!!!!!!!!!"

And then I realized that Rigel's an actual star. Oof.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y'know, I usually balk a bit at SCPs written from really out-there chronological perspectives, so you'd think that something like this, written from the point of view of a far-future organization with no (immediately apparent) link to the Foundation, would bounce right off me.

But I really, really love this one. It's maybe my favorite "end of the world" SCP around. It does so much with implication while leading up to a fantastic and fun reveal at the end, and I think it has just the right balance of cynicism and optimism.

Good stuff.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I'm okay with all of these, except for the 1050 one (that was always meant to be an easter egg) and the 1202 one, as that has nothing to do with SCP-1202, and is just a number I pulled out of a hat.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timers expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

The article makes pretty blatant references to SCP-173, SCP-914, SCP-882, SCP-2000, SCP-1202, SCP-1265, and SCP-1050 in each of the pictograms. My suggestion is to make the item number in the pictogram an in-line link. As such, I am suggesting the following (Example, the rest of the above would look similar):

Editing this:

Pictograph for Item "173"

To this:

Pictograph for Item "173"

Editing this:

Pictograph for Item "914"

To this:

Pictograph for Item "914"

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded:
It's not just that the system is binary. It's also the encoding in it. Without knowing the encryption method used, a binary message will just look like an ordered pattern without context.

A good example of what I mean is morse code. It is effectively binary: a sound and silence. Without knowing what they represent, all you are hearing is a pattern of sounds and silences. With some analysis, you could determine the two states of sound, and that the silence indicates a grouping of long and short sounds.

But then you hit that roadblock. It's clearly artificial, and it likely means something, but what? Lacking knowledge of its encryption, meaning its context, it's virtually impossible to gain any meaningful information from it.

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded: I disagree with all of what you've said. If you researched it, wouldn't you have seen that the molecular formulas and structures for our nucleotides are included in the message?

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded: How the hell would a civilization not know what binary is? Anybody with the sophistication to develop a way to receive and record interstellar radio transmissions would be able to figure it out, even if they didn't know what binary is, because it's not exactly a big leap of logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool story +1 for space invaders!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure the last pictogram means "We can destroy your kind, so keep your Lovecraftian butts away from our dimension."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took'em thousands of years, but they finally got that scarlet bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Destroyer" looks kinda like Reaper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They mention a few times that humans (presumably descended from the 5% of humanity that survived the Destroyer's attack) have spread out to other star systems and become members of galactic society. It's also implied (although never outright stated) that the Protectorate was actually established by the Foundation. That's what the line about the message being in the "Galactic Standard Script" was included for: a hint that the Protectorate are looking at their origins, even if they don't know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but feel remorse over the "It is unknown how the Triumvirate knew of the existence of Galactic Standard Script when creating this pictogram" line. It invokes a "If only we knew that they would use the same script we use" feeling. Some minor questions/feedback:

  • curious as to how 682 would be interpreted in this far future
  • did I miss something, or what happened to the surviving humans?

item.title: Don't use 'Sol' or 'Solar' to describe this star system
item.content:encoded: I don't think any kind of aliens would call our home system as the Solar System with the amount of importance that title gives, when in reality it's just the memory of a dead species (hopefully I understood this scip correctly). Either put a footnote to explain the 'Sol System' is just what the humans called their own system or call it something else (Maybe just a designation, 'Human Star System', or even the 'Terran System' if you want to give it the Sci-fi grove).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly my biggest problem with this is that I think the pictograms could have been much better.
Using cyanide to represent death is fairly clumsy, for example. To someone who has no knowledge of SCP-173, it implies that it produces cyanide… which isn't how it works at all. Also, criticizing SETI for using binary and then using this janky number system is incredibly ironic. I don't hate the number system completely, but it acts really weird when depicting multiples of 10, and it would be a pain to figure out compared to binary. I don't understand why you'd assume base 2 is so difficult to figure out and then use base 10, especially when the base 10 system you're using behaves like it should be base 11…
Of course with pictograms it's difficult to avoid being somewhat ambiguous or cryptic, but I think these could be way less cryptic and ambiguous. And in the case of SCP-173, even if you son't see the ambiguity, the pictogram is very misleading. Finding a good way to depict death would be way better. Imagine a species that isn't affected by cyanide, or doesn't know that humans are, finds this. It's not too difficult to imagine considering the fact that there are plenty of organisms on earth unaffected by cyanide. Why wouldn't they assume SCP-173 just produces lots of cyanide when people aren't looking at it for no reason? Also, I think the 173 pictogram does a poor job of communicating the very simple behavior of 173, but that's a bit more subjective. Personally, I think most of the pictograms do a poor job communicating. One of them has text for Pete's sake! That totally defeats the purpose!

Overall I'm neutral though. If the pictograms were swapped out for better ones, I'd easily +1 and this would be one of my favorite 4000 entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this article. +1. The ending is awesome. It seems so much in character for the Foundation to defeat the destroyer by destroying the Earth.

I think it would be better if you changed the only occurrence of the word "water" to something else. Maybe change:

Due to the high content of dihydrogen monoxide on its surface and atmosphere, 19 satellites acting as water extraction facilities will be deployed in low SCP-4000 orbit.

To something like:

Due to the high content of dihydrogen monoxide on its surface and atmosphere, 19 satellites acting as extraction facilities for the aforementioned substance will be deployed in low SCP-4000 orbit.

I love the effect this article gets from all its uses of "dihydrogen monoxide" - whoever wrote this article clearly doesn't need to consume water to survive. But if the alien(s) writing or translating it knew that they could type "water" instead of "dihydrogen monoxide," why did they bother to write out the longer one every other time?

I think one of the SCP numbers (Earth's or the destroyer's) should be something other than 4000. In my opinion it's a bit of a stretch for both of them to have the same number and for that to be a nice round one like 4000.

item.title: Up-vote.
item.content:encoded:
This is going to sound weird. Is it unique? Yes! Does it fit the theme? Yes! Is the concept full explored? Yes! Is it a SCP-4000 tier article? Yes! Does it deserve an up-vote? Absolutely! Does it leave me wanting more (Even though I understand the concept is fully explored)? No.

Hating to make a Doctor who reference I must. "When all of time and space is your backyard. It just becomes a backyard." This is the scenario I find myself in with your article. It's great and I can't not up vote it. And I can't deny it would make a good 4000 article. When I first read it I openly screamed "YEAH HUMANS!" But then I read the other articles this began to look less and less special. I'm not sure what you can do with this information. Maybe more of those secret messages. Maybe more dread built up for the destroyer. And maybe that white box removed from scp 2000 because it just seems pointless (I understand that this may sound like I'm saying explore more of a concept that I have already established have previously been explored to it's fullest but it really seems more of adding more concept to explore). And maybe some references for other broken SCP's like 1968 being incapable of correcting this timeline although that may be sitting on a concept for too long.

Either way good article :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh all the images on the article seem to be broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed reading this and trying to decipher the pictograms. My only problem is that the ending left me a little unsatisfied. I like the whole ftl projectile earth idea, but what confuses me is the "destroyer." It just seems too arbitrary and vague. What I think would be interesting is some kind of secret link/file containing the foundation's file for SCP-4000 (the destroyer). I still feel like this works really well, so +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

Other beings will have a history of their own, and so having the article be historical from the perspective of the Protectorate is an interesting format screw and certainly fitting of the contest. Additionally, the SCP Foundation turning the entire planet into a massive bullet is pretty clever, and the various pictograms certainly help the piece stand out among its competitors in the contest.

+1. Well done, Ihp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm aware it's glitchy; there's nothing I have no idea how to fix it, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this a lot.

Of note, the final message from the Foundation didn't wrap text on mobile, so some of it got cut off even when I rotated my phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like the concept, not a big fan of this being on the mainlist. Gonna have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As did I…suppose I'm always rooting for us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I took a more optimistic interpretation that it says "One down, many more to go".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually tried the collapsible thing, but I think I fucked with your original code so much that it doesn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I am humbled that I was able to contribute in a small way to this. To be blunt, this article may not have my brain by the end of the contest, but it will have my heart; I closely watched it develop in your sandbox and was blown away with each revision. I am cheerleading this one more than any other, and not because you mention me.

My only suggestion would be to put the typewriter animation into a collapsable, as it will begin when opened. That way, the effect isn’t finished by the time a reader gets to it. This delay only works on certain devices, apparently.

I think your angle on the contest to delve far into the future as a way to explore history is by far the most unique approach I’ve read. I think that the reinterpretation of the Foundation as the “Triumvirate” is a stroke of brilliance. That you wrote this in the short amount of time allotted is deeply impressive.

I disagree that the pacing is negatively impacted by the pictograms. I regret that so much criticism has been aimed at the pictograms; they are after all what some of the brightest minds of a generation regarded as the best hope for distantly-received messages, as far as I understand. That you are getting the short end of that doesn’t seem quite fair. I would love to know how you made them.

item.title: +95 to +87 in less than two minutes.
(no title)
item.content:encoded: This just doesn't click for me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it that unlikely to be lost information, if the Stellar thing was created by humans, though? Even if the signal to noise ratio of their data storage, so to speak, was about 99.99% on a yearly basis, about 98% of all data would be lost on its original form after 40,000 years. Think of it as a very, very long telephone / chinese whispers game.

There would be various reasons to that happening, including server loss and backup loss due to components failing or data being corrupted; war and scarcity; language shift not being accurately reproduced; malicious edits etc.

Or maybe the data is still somewhere but can't be readily accessed by the interested parties; it could have been forgotten, after 40,000 years of stored information piling on top of it, much like modern governments don't know precisely how many laws are there actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like this and will upvote it after the contest if i remember, but the scp itself doesnt really feel like it fits the contest? i would prefer an item that itself has some sort of effect on history (like more conceptually, not just that its involved in some historical event i guess) or is influenced by existing history, rather than an article that is essentially just about the history of an item

edit: actually, thinking about this more, this one does fit the history theme more than some other things that are higher voted, so i take this comment back, but still no upvote right now from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know. Something about this doesn't really sit right with me. The pictures are neat as a concept but I don't know if the story sells as well as some others. I might come back to this one I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really work for me and I've been trying to think of a way to articulate why for over a day now. Feel free to correct me if I get any of the actual plot points here wrong, as I might be misinterpreting things. And, I apologise if this is a little rambley and stream-of-consciousness nonsense; as I said, having trouble articulating my issues here.

Big rambling nonsense
– hide block

So, there's this future space federation thing that is harvesting Earth for water after it was abandoned (40,000 or so years ago, if im not mistaken? Assuming "GSC 10997" is the same as the Galactic Year used later on.) Fair enough. No issues there. My problem is a kind of compound thing, I think. There are a number of hints throughout the article that suggest this space federation was formed by humans (or, maybe even by the Foundation itself), after they abandoned Earth. They have FTL technology at this point, they are aware of life on other planets presumably (due to Saturn apparently being home to robo-people) and thus must be reasonably aware of the importance of long-term planning.

So, why would the Foundation leave behind satellites detailing all this info in such a bizarre format? Describing things pictographically like this is such a minefield of accidental cultural (and even species-specific) meanings that expecting anyone on these kinds of time-scales to ever work out what this are trying to imply is a long-shot at best.

Except, as I said, as far as I can tell this space federation was founded by humans and/or the Foundation itself. Since the Foundation especially should know to be operating on galactic time-scales at this point, and since you do state that humans still exist in the galaxy, why would this history not be documented somewhere? Are we to believe that a solar-system spanning human race would leave their entire history behind after abandoning Earth? That they wouldn't take important things like why Earth was abandoned, the history of their species and its evolution, and (in the case of the Foundation) the entire SCP database with them, and keep it in a format that will survive and be accessible to all humans?

I guess if I try and boil down my issues, I'd state it as this: either the Stellar Congressional Protectorate is entirely alien, in which case I don't buy that they would be able to translate any of this, or they are human/founded by humans, in which case I don't buy that this information would all be lost to them in the first place, even after the huge amounts of time this thing seems to be operating on.

I'm also confused by that last image. Was it send by the Foundation, or by the monster? If it was sent by the Foundation, I don't get why they would sent it at all (or how they would have known about the other monters). If it was sent by the monster - why would it be sending things in that format?

Still, with all of that said, there's a lot I do like here. You've clearly put a lot of thought into the images and their meanings, and the idea of turning Earth itself into an FTL projectile to kill a space monster is a really good one. I can't upvote this because of all the issues I mentioned above, but I also can't bring myself to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

It would make for an interesting tale, but I do not like it as an SCP article and I definitely do not like it as THE SCP-4000 article. I would certainly not care to see more articles from this perspective… I think it works as a one time thing.

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded:
To add over that: even if we know that the aliens could somehow render binary "obsolete" how could we even know what they're using? Like it or not we're still trapped in von Neumann architecture. Quantum computing has only been available in last decade. DNA computing is still far off—and without guarantee aliens would even have DNA. Betting on something that we ourselves haven't used species-wide is more of an oxymoron than betting on the building blocks that let us build the first switch all the way to nano-scale transistor. I think the Arecibo message is not be all end all messages, but a stepping stone to determine if there's someone out there to listen, and then we could start talking with more sophisticated method. It's a baby's cry before the parents come and teach them proper language.

There's a good reason why Morse code is so prevalent and successful.

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded:
Still, SETI made a lot of assumptions in the transmission of the message— like, maybe they don't have our form of DNA on an alien planet, and the idea of a GACT structure is entirely… well, alien to them, and most of them probably aren't going to have any clue what the symbol at the bottom means.

(Also keep in mind I wrote that at midnight, and was giddy from posting this, so my ranting may not be entirely accurate. Apologies for stepping on toes.)

item.title: Re: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded:
To say SETI are idiots for using what is nearly the simplest possible system for encoding information in order to make it as easy as possible for recipients to decode a message is just plain silly. It's not flawed to think aliens would understand a simple system like binary (for that matter, it doesn't make any sense to say binary is something that would be "rendered obsolete"). You're looking at a simple, universal, mathematical concept like binary (referring to a system with two possible states, like the Arecibo message, rather than a binary place-value system) like it's some kind of clumsily developed man-made technology, rather than a simple mathematical concept, like addition.

I liked the SCP though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the inspiration can't be taken away. This lil jewel is the proof of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this to be abominably clever. Probably my favorite of the ones I've read so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean its good on some counts because its basically a distant future S.C.P Foundation trying to unravel what exactly happened to get them to this point. But it suffers heavily from the pictograms and the pacing. Hope this was good advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pacing is definitely improved by this now. My other issues with the article are still present in the core of the article, so I can't upvote, but my downvote is removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pacing suffers from this. The use of displaying pictographs, explaining it, and then analyzing it gets exhausting after a while,

You are completely correct. To be honest, the descriptions were more guidelines for how I would make the pictograms, as opposed to how people would interpret them. The whole point of the pictograms was, originally, that they would be difficult to interpret, and I overestimated how much a future civilization would be able to figure out.

I've adjusted the page accordingly, and added the pictograms for 882 and 2000. I deeply apologize to anyone who's voted on this previously if they are unhappy with this change; if staff feels I should repost the article to reset the votes, I will do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I read this, the more I don't enjoy it.

I get what you're aiming for here, but I'm having a hard time being able to suspend my disbelief for this. While you introduce background for the setting this takes place in, I have a hard time being able to buy into the premise to begin with. The Foundation's long-gone, and this version of them documenting the Foundation in a unique way, as well as the build-up to the Destroyer, ultimately makes me wish I was just reading this from the actual Foundation's perspective instead of a retrospective. (Also, I understand why you used Stellar Congressional Protectorate but I really wish you came up with a name that didn't fit that acronym to fully go-in on your format screw.)

The pacing suffers from this. The use of displaying pictographs, explaining it, and then analyzing it gets exhausting after a while, and it feels like a lot of hoops to jump through in order to get into the meat of the story, which I don't find interesting if we're not seeing it directly unfold from the main viewpoint of the Foundation. At most, it's a curious read for a story that I don't feel pays off. Not to mention, I'm tired of planet-ending monstrous threats in SCP articles.

I initially liked it because of how different it felt with the format screw, but it doesn't pay off for me, and the more I reflect on it, the more I can't get behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole time I was reading this, I was wondering how it could be justified as an actual database entry on the mainlist rather than a tale. I mean, it's mostly about the Foundation, and "SCP" here stands for something entirely different. Then I got to this

seems to be what the Triumvirate termed "SCP-4000"

And that's how.

I'll be honest, I don't get the final pictogram completely, but the story is great and the format screw compelling, so have an upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man. I miss 5700 Years Later.

item.title: I promised myself that I'd give in-depth critic of everything I voted in the 4kon. Sorry for the length. Title included
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… I like the way this story is told. The use of pictograms is really clever; trying to decipher their meaning and implications has been an interesting experience. The slow disclosure of information fits the tale perfectly, and creates a chilling sense of discovery. Yeah, I like the way this story is told.

But, in my opinion, the story itself destroys its own potential by the end. Throughout the article is established the emptiness left by a humanity that fought and lost, the destruction and recreation of all life once and once again, our end at the hands of an eldritch abomination that is so vast and so big that we can't neither defeat nor negotiate with. Nihilism, extinction, inability to avoid being devoured by the God who raised us to that purpose; these themes set a specific tone, the expectation that this story can only resolve in the most ominous way possible.

And then, everything the article has built since the beginning is destroyed to its foundations; the good Stellar Protectorate saves us all and the Foundation destroys the great, death-bringing monster. Everything so carefully established for the duration of the piece means nothing and is worth nothing, the chilling expectation of horror is never met, and instead all is replaced by a good ending completely alien to the rest of the article that is far less interesting and thought provoking. The last bit of horror is taken away when the eldritch abomination is called "Yog-Sothoth wannabe".

"But there are many more like it out there! Perhaps millions!" Yeah, poor guys, we have cool FTL ships and an almighty Galactic Coalition willing to protect us :P

That's how I feel about this SCP. English is not my primary language; I apologize if something in this message sounds strange or weird.

I have to -1, but I'll be watching for the future additions author has promised. Don't get me wrong, it's a great skip, and it'll be loved by many — it's just that I don't like how the story twists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow, good, you just kill one of us, one of thousands'.

Thank you! I was hoping that would come across to people. I had to explain the other pictograms because they were really esoteric— even when making them, I sometimes forgot my own rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really simple type of format-screw, an awesome form to use images, and a cool history of 'if I die, you're coming with me'. But the thing I most like about this, is the last image saying 'wow, good, you just kill one of us, one of thousands'. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using the Earth as a light speed projectile. I give you all my upvotes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea of going into the distqnf future, and looking back to how humanity went extinct. Also, spoiler, the skips caused a XK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m not the biggest fan of the format screw here, but it’s still a very entertaining skip that I enjoyed throughout. As always, great work, Ihp.

+1

item.title: In the very distant future, "What is Sunday?" AD...
item.content:encoded:
So this is still a little unfinished— I had issues uploading a pair of the pictographs (Issue on my end, internet and computer has been screwy because of air conditioners running at full blast) and may be forced to host them on a site like Imgur in the next couple of days. I'll try to localize most of them before the 4th.

Work on this started literally eight or so minutes after the 4000 contest was announced. I thought "Hey, the SETI Arecibo message is pretty cool. Might be neat to base an SCP around a future civilization discovering the Foundation's equivalent of it."

And let me tell you, in the course of writing and researching this, I've found out something: SETI. Are. Idiots. The Arecibo image is based on the flawed assumption that Aliens are going to know what binary is— for all we know, a civilization it reaches may have invented radio to pick it up, but may not have developed binary. Maybe they've made a more efficient system that's rendered binary obsolete.

That aside, coming up with pictograms to depict most of this stuff was fun, and challenging. Probably the hardest to covey was the one from 173— there's no universal sign for "kill". Some worms can survive with their heads cut off, for instance. So, I chose potassium cyanide as a substance that's almost universally toxic to carbon-based life to convey "oh shit, this thing kills people".
I see you reading between the lines. Look at SCP-1050. One of my favorite SCPs, for what the destroyer is.
Credit where credit is due: Toa_Vine, Sir Baubius, Dog Teeth, Rimple, and Anaxagoras all critted this, and scpcrnp graciously let me use their code from SCP-sqrt-1-J for this article. Several more may have critted; If I have forgotten you, feel free to say so.

Arecibo message image taken from Wikimedia commons. All other images of my own creation.

E: Also I just realized that this is my 101st article. Woo!
